title: Olearia incana
intro: Olearia incana, also known as the silvertop daisy bush, is a shrub native to Australia that belongs to the Asteraceae family. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with potential health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which may aid in wound healing and the management of infections. The bioactive constituents of Olearia incana include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Olearia incana is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are believed to promote digestive health and alleviate symptoms of fever and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Olearia incana is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It is native to New Zealand and is commonly known as the hoary tree daisy or hoary olearia. The classification of Olearia incana is within the genus Olearia, which contains over 200 species of shrubs and small trees. Olearia incana is further classified as part of the tribe Gnaphalieae and the subfamily Gnaphalioideae. Its classification is also recognized as Asteraceae sensu lato, which is a broader classification of the daisy family.
uses: Olearia incana is traditionally used in New Zealand's Maori culture for its medicinal properties. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. The plant's leaves are used in various remedies, including treating wounds, colds, and fever. Additionally, Olearia incana is also used to relieve coughs and sore throats. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Olearia incana for medicinal purposes.

Further research is needed to fully confirm the health benefits of Olearia incana, but it has shown promise in traditional medicine.
